items.description: Public type `Greeter` with `greet(name: String) -> String` returning "Hello, <name>!" and `greet() -> String` returning "Hello, world!".

rules_checked.reasoning: Declared kind is 'type' and the item describes a concrete Swift type (Greeter) with two methods (greet(name:), greet()) — this is exactly what a 'type' item should describe, not a protocol, function, or module. Matches.

Target Sources/Greeter/Greeter.swift places the Greeter type inside the Greeter target as declared in Package.swift (.target(name: "Greeter")). The existing Sources/Greeter/GreeterModule.swift confirms this is the correct module home for feature types; no other module is more appropriate.
This is a plain library type with pure string-formatting logic, not a view or view-model; there is no view-layer domain logic or data access from a view model at stake. No altitude violation applies.

Sources/Greeter currently only contains GreeterModule.swift, an empty namespace marker enum with no functionality. Introducing a new Greeter.swift file for the actual Greeter type is warranted rather than overloading the namespace marker file; granularity is appropriate and keeps a single responsibility (greeting logic) in its own file.
The item introduces a concrete type only, no protocol is proposed. No speculative abstraction is being added, so there is nothing to justify or reject here — compliant by absence.

Greeter is a plain, descriptive noun for a type; greet(name:) and greet() are verb-named methods per Swift API Design Guidelines (mutating/non-mutating action reads as a command), with greet() as a natural overload providing a default world greeting. Names carry appropriate semantics for their kind.

The item declares no imports and no cross-module dependencies; it's a self-contained type within its own module with no smuggled forbidden import indicated in the description or existing package graph.
